_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.

clinical_thresholds.note: For surgical management, inverse of non-surgical criteria

common_queries: CT brain report not suggestive of depressed fracture
Clinical notes detailing injury missing or incomplete at pre-authorization

Detailed Discharge Summary missing at claim submission or missing follow-up date

clinical_key_pointers: Depressed skull fractures result from high-energy direct blow, often to the frontoparietal region. They can be open (skin laceration or communication with paranasal sinuses/middle ear) or closed, and may involve associated intracranial injuries like epidural hematoma, dural tears, and seizures.
CT scan is the gold standard for diagnosis of skull fractures, with thinly sliced bone windows (1-1.5 mm) and sagittal reconstruction being useful.
Skull films are suboptimal for basilar skull fractures and generally not beneficial when a CT scan is obtained.
MRI is ancillary for ligamentous and vascular injuries; bony injuries are better visualized with CT.
Complete neurological examination, baseline laboratory analyses, and tetanus toxoid (for open fractures) are part of the diagnostic workup.
Indications for CT in TBI include: Witnessed LOC (Loss of Consciousness).
Indications for CT in TBI include: Definite Amnesia.
Indications for CT in TBI include: Disorientation with a Glasgow Coma Scale score (GCS) of 13-15.
Indications for CT in TBI include: GCS <15 at 2 hours after injury.
Indications for CT in TBI include: Suspected open or depressed fracture.
Indications for CT in TBI include: Any signs of basilar skull fracture (Raccoon eye, Battle sign, Otorrhea or Rhinorrhea).
Indications for CT in TBI include: Vomiting of > 2 episodes.
Indications for CT in TBI include: Age > 65 years.
Indications for CT in TBI include: LOC for > 5 minutes.
Indications for CT in TBI include: Mechanism of injury (as a general trigger).
surgical_options: Surgery to elevate depressed skull fractures is preferred in: Open (compound) fractures.
Surgery to elevate depressed skull fractures is preferred in: Depressed fracture thickness > of calvaria.
Surgery to elevate depressed skull fractures is preferred in: Those fractures not meeting criteria for non-surgical management.
additional_information.comorbidity_management: Seizure medications are recommended if the chance of developing seizures is higher than 20%. Open fractures, if contaminated, may require antibiotics in addition to tetanus toxoid.
